onboard.title: Welcome to Data360 News Agent
onboard.intro: Newsworthy facts from Data360 indicators, verified and ready to publish.

newsletter.title: Daily LAC newsletter
newsletter.intro: One finding per day, ready to publish. Each morning the agent reviews Data360 indicators for Latin America and the Caribbean, picks the one most worth a story — by magnitude of change, absence of media coverage and regional weight — and delivers it with a verified narrative and direct link to source.
newsletter.feature_1: Flagship piece: orphan indicators. Data that updated and no outlet covered. "12 data points changed and nobody reported them."
newsletter.feature_2: Inline verification: every figure in the email links to the exact Data360 dataset row behind it. If the claim cannot survive a click, it does not ship.
newsletter.feature_3: Built for Latin American and Caribbean newsrooms. Free for newsrooms and NPOs in developing countries, always.
newsletter.audience: The daily send and coverage of 12,000 indicators are built on the current demo — we start with a bounded LAC focus and grow coverage.

about.title: About Data360 News Agent
about.lead: Data360 News Agent is an AI news agency that watches, understands and contextualizes World Bank indicators — and delivers each finding as a verified piece with local perspective, ready for a newsroom to edit and publish. This demo is the pilot case of the software, built by Abrimos.info for the Data360 Global Challenge.
about.what_body: The agent follows economic, social and governance indicators in five countries in the region (Argentina, Ecuador, Guatemala, Honduras, Mexico). Every time the World Bank updates an indicator, the agent reviews the series for statistically notable changes and, when it finds them, contextualizes them for the country and writes a story. The result is two formats: Stories about a single indicator and Features that connect several. Every numeric claim in the text is linked inline to its exact row on data360.worldbank.org — readers can verify with one click.
about.scope_body: In this demo, the agent applies two detection strategies: abrupt changes (z-score over the historical series) and cross-country anomalies (a country outside the regional pattern). The watchlist includes ~35 indicators in three rhythms — pulse (sub-annual), annual and forecast — for the five countries. Processing is replay over historical snapshots: the algorithm is production-grade, but detected facts are changes that already happened, recreated for the demo.
about.method_body: For each catalogue indicator, the agent: (1) downloads the historical series from Data360; (2) detects statistically relevant change; (3) builds numbered context with indicator methodology, country series, regional comparables, local press headlines, editorial rules and candidates; (4) drafts the piece with Reuters Handbook + AP Stylebook anchors and country → LAC → world narrative axis; (5) validates with an auto-verification protocol and marks every figure with inline traceability; (6) publishes to the country feed and per-story chat. If a figure fails at any step, the piece does not ship.
about.feature_1: Country home page with featured report, headlines and freshly-updated indicators.
about.feature_2: Bilingual story (ES/EN) with series sparkline and inline Data360 traceability.
about.feature_3: Data period highlighted; chronological order by observation period.
about.feature_4: Local press from the country, integrated into agent context via GDELT.
about.feature_5: Per-story chat to dig deeper into data and sources; exportable to Markdown or PDF.
about.verify_body: Every piece ships with its proof. At the foot of the article: link to the Data360 dataset, CSV download, and indicator methodology reference. In the text: every figure is a claim token pointing to the exact context block the model used. This is not semantic fact-checking — it is mechanical traceability: open the token and you see where the number comes from.
about.pcn_intro: Open any story from the agent and you will notice some numbers are underlined. That is not decoration: it is inline traceability. Each underlined figure links to the exact Data360 dataset block behind it.
about.pcn_list_title: Tap a figure and you will see:
about.pcn_li_1: The exact indicator (with its Data360 code).
about.pcn_li_2: The period the observation belongs to.
about.pcn_li_3: The original value, without agent rounding.
about.pcn_li_4: A link to the dataset page on data360.worldbank.org and to the CSV download.
about.pcn_fail: If the figure does not survive the click, the story is not published.
about.pcn_mechanical: This layer is not semantic fact-checking — the agent does not judge whether an official statement is "true" or "false". It is more modest and more useful: mechanical traceability. Every number travels with its proof.
about.pcn_lib: The component is called PCN (Proof-Carrying Numbers) and is an open-source library from the World Bank itself. We integrate it into the pipeline; we did not invent it.
about.pcn_demo_link: See a story with active traceability →
about.limit_1: Coverage: ~35 indicators in 5 countries, not all 12,000 / 200+ in Data360.
about.limit_2: Pace: replay over historical snapshots, not continuous monitoring.
about.limit_3: Infrastructure: local CSV data; no ingestion orchestration or canonical storage.
about.limit_4: Press language: headlines via GDELT in Spanish only.
about.limit_5: Newsletter: under construction (functional MVP planned for Challenge launch).
about.limit_6: Chat latency: deep per-indicator analysis can take minutes.
about.limit_7: All of the above is on the public roadmap in the GitHub repository.
about.roadmap_body: Next steps: detect divergences between official discourse and data; continuous monitoring with orchestrated ingestion (Apache NiFi) and canonical storage (OpenSearch); full coverage of 12,000 indicators; B2B open-source replicability for data publishers.
about.abrimos_body: This demo was developed by Abrimos.info, a virtual distributed organization founded in Mexico working from and for Latin America with a global perspective. We build digital infrastructure with verifiable information in service of the region's democracies. We combine open-data experience with narrative, investigative, and software development capabilities. We support organizations, governments, and newsrooms turning data into decision inputs and backed narratives. We have years of collaboration with regional newsrooms and active agreements with several.
about.team_body: Three people based in LAC built this demo.
about.team_edu_name: Eduard Martín-Borregón — Lead, narrative and UX
about.team_edu_bio: Executive Director of Abrimos.info. Mozilla Fellow 2024–2026, Sigma Award 2020. 15+ years in data journalism and civic technology in Latin America. On this project: defines the product, writes site copy, and produces Challenge materials.
about.team_martin_name: Martín Szyszlican — LLM agents, RAG, and dashboard
about.team_martin_bio: CTO of Abrimos.info. Co-founder of QuiénEsQuién.Wiki. 10+ years building open-source infrastructure for transparency, including TorreDeControl and MexicoLeaks. On this project: designs agentic architecture, integrates language models, and builds the agent frontend.
about.team_fer_bio: Chief Data Officer of Abrimos.info. Architect of TeseoETL, the open-source engine we use at Sociedad.info and Plaza Pública. 15+ years in journalistic data pipelines. On this project: designs the Data360 connector, normalizes the catalogue, and defines the indicator schema.
about.team_experience: Abrimos.info is a civic technology organization based in Mexico building independent digital infrastructure for democratic accountability. We reach more than 80,000 unique monthly users through Sociedad.info, Pídala.info, Gobernantes.info, and Amnesia.lat.
about.ai_title: The role of AI in these stories
about.ai_intro: Every piece this agent publishes is written by a language model. No human editor reads each story before it goes out. That is a deliberate choice — not an oversight — and it is worth explaining.
about.ai_why_body: The value of an automated agency is operating at a scale no human team can sustain. If every piece went through an editor before publishing, the agent would stop being an agency and become a writing assistant.
about.ai_compensate_title: How we compensate for the absence of a human editor
about.ai_comp_1: Explicit editorial anchors — the agent is bound to the Reuters Handbook of Journalism (structure, attribution, active voice) and the AP Stylebook (micro-style, numbers, acronyms). Not a loose LLM; an LLM with journalistic discipline encoded in the prompt.
about.ai_comp_2: Mandatory narrative axis country → LAC → world — every piece must place the reader first in their country, then in the region, then in the world. Without that, the validator blocks it.
about.ai_comp_3: Mechanical traceability for every figure — readers can verify with one click. If the model gets a number wrong, the original source is one tap away and the piece loses credibility instead of adding invented figures to the ecosystem.
about.ai_comp_4: Q1–Q12 protocol blocking pieces with unsourced figures, unlabeled inferences, news citations without URL, or missing any of the three narrative axes. Full detail in
about.ai_comp_5: Foot notice on every piece about the role of AI — readers always know what they are reading.
about.ai_newsroom_title: What this means for a newsroom using these pieces
about.ai_newsroom_1: Do not republish verbatim — use as input for your own story with human editorial judgment. How to use these stories explains how.
about.ai_newsroom_2: Verify the figures — the system makes it easy; the last step is the journalist's.
about.ai_newsroom_3: Cite the original source — the Data360 indicator, not this agent.
about.ai_roadmap: The roadmap includes an optional human review layer for newsrooms that need publish-ready pieces without intermediate steps. If your outlet is interested, write to contacto@abrimos.info.
about.license_code: Code: open source under GPLv3 — designed so any institution publishing data catalogues can install it on their own.
about.license_content: Published content: CC BY 4.0 (see How to use these stories).

home.hero.title: A news agency for the World Bank's Data360.
home.hero.subtitle: Verified figures, framed for your country, ready to publish.
home.hero.body: Every time the World Bank updates one of its 12,000 indicators, this agent reviews the series, finds what's worth a story, compares it across the region, and writes the piece. Every figure in the text links to the exact row of the dataset behind it — readers click and see the source.
picker.title: Choose an edition
picker.recent_lead: Whenever the World Bank updates an indicator, the agent processes the change. (In this demo, daily batch; in production the target is hourly.)

static.not_found.contact: If you got here from an external link, tell us which one: contacto@abrimos.info. Probably a piece we moved and we'd like you to reach where you were going.

alerts.sample_empty: No recent indicators to show in this sample yet.
static.metodologia.title: Methodology
static.metodologia.lead: How the agent decides what's news, writes the story, and validates before publishing.
static.metodologia.body: The agent runs two layers: statistical analysis (abrupt changes and cross-country anomalies) and editorial writing (Stories and Features) with numbered omnibus context, Reuters + AP anchors, country → LAC → world narrative axis, and Q1–Q12 validation.
static.privacidad.title: Privacy policy
static.privacidad.body: This demo does not use tracking cookies or third-party analytics. If you subscribe, we store your email and subscription type in a local TSV file. Chat questions are processed by an external LLM; we do not associate them with your identity. Contact: contacto@abrimos.info.
static.terminos.title: Terms of use
static.terminos.body: Data360 News Agent is an automated news agency operated by Abrimos.info as a Data360 Global Challenge demo. Published content is CC BY 4.0; code is GPLv3. Figures have mechanical traceability to Data360; editorial interpretation is generated automatically.
static.uso.title: How to use these stories
static.uso.body: Stories and features are under Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 (CC BY 4.0). You may copy, translate and adapt them citing the agent, the Data360 indicator, and noting any edits.
article.disclaimer_intro_before: This piece was drafted by an artificial intelligence agent from the Data360 indicator cited below. The agent follows style anchors from the Reuters Handbook of Journalism and the AP Stylebook, narrates from the lead country outward to the region and the world, and runs the piece through an automated validation protocol (Q1–Q12 —
article.disclaimer_ai_did_body: read the indicator's series, detected the change, built context (historical data, regional comparables, local press), wrote the narrative, tagged every figure with its source, and validated the piece against the dataset.
article.disclaimer_ai_not_label: What the AI did not do:
article.disclaimer_verify: Figures in the text are underlined — tap any of them to verify against the source data.
article.reuse_body: Published under CC BY 4.0 — copy, translate, adapt. Just cite the agent and the Data360 indicator.
article.newsletter_title: Want a verified finding in your inbox every morning?
article.newsletter_body: A daily email for LAC newsrooms: the day's most relevant change among indicators the agent follows. One figure, its regional context, and the link to the dataset.
article.newsletter_privacy: No spam. Unsubscribe from any email.
pcn.tooltip_body: Each underlined figure links to the exact Data360 row behind it. Tap to verify.
